blocks.text: Your navel is a rounded bowl that never lacks mixed wine. Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.

2 : Parts of the body not normally exposed to view are described in metaphors that are not transparent. Perhaps is a euphemism for “vulva.” suggests the softness and gentle curve of the woman's stomach.
Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
Your neck is like an ivory tower. Your eyes are pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim. Your nose is like a tower of Lebanon, overlooking Damascus.

4 : The image evokes the gracefulness of a long neck. , the same Hebrew word, “ʿayin,” means both “eye” and “spring.” , east of Jerusalem in Transjordan, where excavations have revealed the remains of a large reservoir, dating from the ninth to eighth centuries . The location of , meaning “daughter of many,” is unknown. Perhaps the significance of has to do with its scent; cf. 4.11n.
Your head crowns you like Carmel, and your flowing locks are like purple; a king is held captive in the tresses.

5 : The comparison to Mount Carmel, which overlooks the Mediterranean Sea at Haifa, suggests the stately way she holds her head. , shiny black hair has a purple sheen. Purple, the color of royalty, evokes rich and luxurious hair, suitable for capturing a .
How fair and pleasant you are, O loved one, delectable maiden!
You are stately as a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.
I say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its branches. O may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples,
and your kisses like the best wine that goes down smoothly, gliding over lips and teeth.
6.4–7.9: The man's second long speech. Again the man describes the woman's charms in similes and metaphors.

7–9 : , i.e., “I intend to climb.” The man puts himself in the picture (cf. 5.1), and, through a series of images, moves closer to the object of his desire. First he must climb the tall to reach that represent his lover's breasts (v. 7). Then, envisioned as grape clusters (v. 8), her breasts become more accessible.
I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me.
Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the fields, and lodge in the villages;
11 : , the same Hebrew word also means “henna bushes,” preferable here because of the outdoor setting.
let us go out early to the vineyards, and see whether the vines have budded, whether the grape blossoms have opened and the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.
The mandrakes give forth fragrance, and over our doors are all choice fruits, new as well as old, which I have laid up for you, O my beloved.
7.10–13: The woman's reply. The man's second long speech, like his first, gives rise to the woman's invitation to love (cf. 4.16).
13 : were thought by some to be an aphrodisiac.
